author: Robert Carr
description: A no-cook snack plate with cured meat, cheese, bread, pickles, greens, onion, and a simple Dijon pickle mayo for building balanced bites.
ingredients: 1 to 2 slices ciabatta or wheat sandwich bread, cut into pieces
1 to 2 ounces prosciutto, salami, chorizo, or a mix
1 to 2 ounces cheddar or whole-milk mozzarella, sliced
A few dill pickle slices or spears
Small handful baby arugula or lettuce, patted dry
A few very thin onion slices, optional
1 teaspoon pickle brine from the jar
instructions: Stir the mayonnaise, Dijon mustard, and pickle brine together in a small bowl to make a tangy dip.
Cut or tear the bread into bite-size pieces. Toast the bread first if you want it crisp, or leave it soft if you want the fastest version.
Slice the cheese into pieces that fit on the bread.
Fold or tear the cured meat into loose pieces instead of leaving it in flat sheets.
Arrange the cured meat, cheese, bread, pickles, greens, onion, and dip on a plate.
Build each bite with bread, a small piece of cheese, a folded piece of meat, a little pickle, and a leaf or two of greens.
Use the Dijon pickle mayo lightly as a dip or spread so the salty meat and cheese still come through.
notes: Yes, these ingredients can be eaten cold as long as they have been stored properly and smell/taste normal.
For one person, choose 1 cured meat and 1 cheese instead of opening every package. The plate tastes cleaner and the opened packages stay fresher.
The cured meats and cheeses are salty and rich, so the pickles, greens, onion, and mustard dip make the plate taste more balanced.
Small bites work better than giant stacks. Too much meat in one bite can taste salty before the cheese, bread, and pickles have a chance to balance it.
Use smaller amounts of the spicy chorizo or salty prosciutto at first, then add more if you like the flavor.
If the onion tastes too sharp, use only a few slices or skip it.
title: Cured Meat Cheese Snack Plate
